DEFENDANT H MART SAN FRANCISCO LLC'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND STAY PROCEEDINGS IS GRANTED. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Oct-17-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Rochelle Calderon, Esq.310-284-3880 Barnes & Thornburg LLP for Defendant H mart.
No other Appearance.

Discussed. Counsel informs the Court she submits on the tentative ruling. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT H MART SAN FRANCISCO LLC'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings.

Defendant H Mart San Francisco LLC's motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is granted.

It is the burden of the party seeking to compel arbitration to establish the existence of an enforceable arbitration agreement. (Diaz v. Sohnen Enterprises (2019) 34 Cal.App.5th 126, 129.) "A movant can bear this initial burden 'by attaching a copy of the arbitration agreement purportedly bearing the opposing party's signature.'" (Iyere v. Wise Auto Group (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 747, 755, citing Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1060.)

Here, H Mart attached a copy of the arbitration agreement bearing plaintiff Henriquez's signature. (Declaration of Yoon Hee Kim in Support of Motion to Compel, Exhibit A.) Henriquez argues that H Mart failed to prove he agreed to arbitration because of a defect in Yoon Hee Kim's declaration. (Plaintiff's Opposition, 4:8-7:2.) The declaration explains the nature of Kim's employment at H Mart and her familiarity with H Mart's records and corporate operations. (Declaration of Yoon Hee Kim, Paragraphs 2-4.) These statements provide substantial evidence to support that Ms. Kim has personal knowledge of the matters set forth in her declaration (Preciado v. Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp., (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 964, 974-75.) The declaration also properly authenticates the attached exhibits via the business records exception. (Declaration of Yoon Hee Kim, Paragraph 6.) This is enough for H Mart to bear its initial burden to prove that a valid arbitration agreement exists.

In opposition, Henriquez alleges that he did not sign the agreement and that the signature differs from his. (Declaration of Edenilson Carmona Henriquez; Paragraph 5.) This is contradicted by the numerous documents that Henriquez signed alongside the agreement and that H Mart submits as evidence in its reply. (Declaration of Judi H. Seun; Paragraph 3; Exhibit A.) The declaration submitted in reply also rebuts Henriquez's claims that he was not provided access to Spanish translation of the documents, and Henriquez does not claim that he asked for a translation in his declaration. (Id., Paragraphs 4-5; See Caballero v. Premier Care Simi Valley LLC (2021) 69 Cal.App.5th 512, 519.) In opposition, Henriquez does not explicitly rebut H Mart's argument that the agreement meets the Armendariz factors and is not unconscionable, and therefore concedes these points. Henriquez also argues that the FAA does not apply to the contract and that Labor Code 229 renders the agreement unenforceable. (Plaintiff's Opposition, 7:3-9:16.) However, as H Mart notes, the agreement explicitly provides that it is governed by the FAA, which is enough to apply the FAA to the agreement and preempt Labor Code 229. (Cronus Invs., Inc. v. Concierge Servs. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 376, 383; Performance Team Freigh Systems, Inc. v. Aleman (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 1233, 1240.)

For these reasons, the motion to compel arbitration is granted and the proceedings are stayed, including Henriquez's representative PAGA claim.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
